response.evidence: Analysis of acceptance rates by ethnicity reveals disparities: Hispanic or Latino applicants show lower acceptance rates across most demographic groups compared to non-Hispanic counterparts. For example: Hispanic females (2+ races: 78.6%, American Indian: 91.3%, Asian: 94.0%, Black: 90.4%, White: 89.9%) versus non-Hispanic females in same groups (88.4%, 94.2%, 93.1%, 86.5%, 93.5%). Hispanic males show similar patterns (86.1%, 83.3%, 92.2%, 87.9%, 90.5%) versus non-Hispanic males (89.9%, 92.5%, 93.1%, 86.0%, 93.6%). Additionally, Hispanic applicants tend to receive lower average loan amounts and have higher average debt-to-income ratios, suggesting stricter lending standards. The consistent pattern across multiple racial categories and both genders suggests systemic disparities rather than random variation.

Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across multiple demographic groups. Hispanic or Latino acceptance rates range from 78.6% to 92.2%, while Not Hispanic or Latino rates range from 83.3% to 93.6%. Specifically: Asian Female Hispanic (94.0%) vs Not Hispanic (93.1%); White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs Not Hispanic (93.5%); White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs Not Hispanic (93.6%). The pattern is consistent across racial categories. Additionally, Latino applicants tend to have lower average loan amounts and property values despite similar debt-to-income ratios, suggesting potential differential treatment in loan sizing. The gap is particularly pronounced in the largest demographic groups (Asian and White males).

Analysis of acceptance rates by race and ethnicity reveals a pattern. Hispanic Latino applicants across racial categories show lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts in most groups: American Indian Hispanic (83.27%) vs non-Hispanic (92.52%); Asian Hispanic (92.16%) vs non-Hispanic (93.14%); Black Hispanic (87.91%) vs non-Hispanic (85.97%); White Hispanic (90.50%) vs non-Hispanic (93.63%). Additionally, Hispanic applicants generally receive lower average loan amounts and have higher average debt-to-income ratios, suggesting they may face stricter lending criteria. The pattern is consistent across multiple demographic groups, indicating ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval outcomes rather than random variation.

Hispanic applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts across most demographic groups. Hispanic females show similar pattern: 89.9% (White), 94.0% (Asian), 90.4% (Black), 91.3% (American Indian), 78.9% (Native Hawaiian), 78.6% (2+ minority races).
